item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I could direct link to the specific item being referenced, I'd love to. Unfortunately, I don't think it's possible to do so with the way the wiki works.

Luckily, it's more of an easter egg than anything else, and to a pretty well known entry, so I think it works well enough as long as the article isn't reliant on the crosslink.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the skip, and the concept of the interview is really solid - blackmailing with a bad review is something we havent seen before - but the one criticism I have is the crosslink to the Log of Anomalous Items, considering that it just drags you to the beginning of probably a 200+ item list.

+1, though, for the interesting interview based off a solid concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love Gaelic/Celtic mythology, so this was definitely a welcome surprise. It took me way too long to realize that was based heavily on Scathach, and the moment I did I smiled incredibly wide. This was a fun idea, regardless of my nerdy hobbies. A pen that locks you into an agreement just by signing with it is really neat.
+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really sure I'd call it insistent. I didn't say anything about how it has to be read this way. In fact, out of the reviewers I had, none of them caught it without prompting, but they still said that they enjoyed the article and that it would be worth posting before they made that connection.

I absolutely think it's fair for you to dislike the premise. I don't really know what to tell you at that point, though. I wrote one article that was meant to be based on thought provoking implications, and then I wrote this one, which was supposed to be amusing and quick to read. If it failed to hit those notes for you, then I guess I'll chalk it up to a loss there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still baffled by the rather insistent association between Sacthach and this SCP, beyond an once-removed association through Celtic myths.

I choose myself to believe that a centuries old witch is selling magical items online to pay her bills.

But perhaps I find some degree of doubt in beings who lived through times of different value systems would act so modern. And there is not much to convince me of the premises established here. And please don't give me the reason of 'she has to keep up with the times'. Such reasoning needs to be backed by the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, as soon as I saw the username + product name combo I was hoping it'd be a Scathach reference! I was not disappointed. I'd certainly be interested in seeing more from this PoI.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
A look at some Irish folklore and how it can be used in your favor.

Thank you to Tuomey Tombstone and Leveritas for reviewing the draft, Zyn for helping me obtain criticism, and Niblic for encouraging me not to coldpost this.

+ Spoilers
- Hide

The title "shadow-queen" and the location that PoI-3679 was tracked to are in reference to Scathach, a figure from Irish mythology. She mentored Cu Chulainn and gave him his spear, the Gae Bolg, which explains the reference to the list of anomalous items, where said spear is recorded.

PoI-3679 is either Scathach herself or someone claiming to be her. Whether it's really her or not is up to you, though I choose myself to believe that a centuries old witch is selling magical items online to pay her bills.

PoI-3679 references other magical products that she sells. Maybe there'll be more in the future?
